divergences.reasoning: FINAL RULING: Both teachers agree the post-second-throw interval is the dominant efficiency collapse, but they place the throw-to-cut boundary differently because severe tangling obscures when tying ends and trimming begins. The consensus keeps most of that disputed interval in third_throw while reserving a substantial cut phase.

FINAL RULING: Claude includes nearly the full post-knot tail-management interval in suture_cut, while GPT-4o counts only the instrument-on-tail trimming portion. Consensus assigns 105s to cutting to reflect the clearly prolonged finishing sequence without collapsing the entire disputed interval into cut alone.

FINAL RULING: Both teachers agree placement was not the main failure mode and visibility limits precise measurement. Consensus adopts the rubric-strict 3.0mm total deviation because Claude's extra penalty load came from an out-of-rubric quality adjustment, not clearly worse mark placement.

knot_assessments.notes: First throw is accepted as a surgeon's knot. The lead hand is clearer in GPT-4o's review, while both teachers agree the throw ultimately seated securely despite prolonged handling.
Second throw appears to alternate correctly. Duration remains elevated because long tails and repeated regrasping reduced economy of motion.
Third throw and the transition into trimming are the most ambiguous interval in the recording, but both teachers agree the final knot stack remained intact and secure.

suture_placement.placement_notes: Placement remained close enough to target marks that it should not be treated as the main score driver. Consensus uses the rubric-strict 3.0mm total deviation because visibility is limited and the large Claude delta comes from a separate quality penalty.

drain_assessment.assessment_note: Both teachers agree the drain remained mounted, the slit appears closed, and the final knot is centered with short residual tails despite the prolonged manipulation.

consensus_score.technique_summary: V28 is best read as an equipment-adaptation failure rather than a true skill regression. Needle placement remained serviceable after the station change, but severe suture tangling collapsed efficiency across the throw-to-cut interval. The final knot is still centered, secure, and leaves the drain closed without avulsion, which supports treating this as a station-specific baseline rather than a new performance ceiling.
improvement_suggestions: Confirm whether the station also changed suture material; the visible color change and tangling pattern are consistent with unfamiliar handling characteristics.
If this configuration will be used again, run several dedicated warmup repetitions before any scored attempt so tail management on the new setup normalizes.
Define the cut phase operationally as cutter-entry plus tail trimming in future scoring prompts to reduce the large throw-versus-cut boundary drift seen here.
Do not compare V28 linearly against V26-V27 as if they were the same environment; treat it as an equipment-change baseline.
strengths: Completed the task despite the most difficult equipment-adjustment attempt in the series.
Final knot appears secure and centered with the slit approximated.
Drain integrity was maintained throughout prolonged manipulation with no avulsion.
Core needle-driving skill carried over well enough that placement itself was not the primary failure mode.

critique_reasoning: FINAL ARBITRATION: Exact bottom-line FLS agreement between teachers makes the main arbitration problem one of bookkeeping rather than outcome. Consensus preserves the rubric-strict placement penalty, rejects Claude's out-of-rubric efficiency penalty, and redistributes the disputed third-throw versus cut interval into a blended timeline that reflects severe tangling without forcing a false precision at the phase boundary. The automated Anthropic arbitration path was unavailable because the configured API key was invalid, so this consensus was resolved directly from the two teacher scorecards and the teacher comparison artifact.
